title: Impact of Surface Treatment on Noise in PL-Measurements of Silicon Vacancies in 4H-SiC Lateral pin-Diodes
authors: Jannik H. Schwarberg, Fabian Magerl, Susanne Beuer, Alexander May, Christian Gobert, Martin Siebert, Christian Miersch, Heino Möller, Wolfgang Knolle, Chihang Luo, Jan F. Dick, Franziska C. Beyer, Mathias Rommel, Jörg Schulze
abstract: Silicon vacancies ($V_\mathrm{Si}$) in 4H-SiC are promising candidates for quantum technologies due to their long spin coherence times and integrability into mature semiconductor platforms. However, conventional CMOS-compatible processing introduces significant photoluminescence noise from passivation layers and crystal damage, degrading color center coherence and excitation linewidths. This work evaluates strategies to minimize such background noise. Thermally grown oxides with nitrogen monoxide annealing provide excellent low-noise passivation, remaining stable during subsequent $600\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ thermal treatments. Furthermore, combining reactive ion etching with atomic layer etching eliminates ion-induced surface damage. Into lateral pin-diodes, used for stark shift and photoluminescent excitation linewidth tuning, a selectively etched optical window is integrated. These devices show ideal electrical properties -- blocking up to $150\,\mathrm{V}$ with leakage current below $10\,\mathrm{pA}/μ\mathrm{m}$ -- while significantly enhancing the $V_\mathrm{Si}$ environment. Single emitters in these pin-diodes show an increased signal-to-noise ratio of 15 for near-surface and of 50 for deeper emitters on both c-plane and a-plane wafers.
summary: This paper investigates how different semiconductor fabrication techniques impact the background noise of silicon vacancy emitters in 4H-SiC. By replacing plasma-based deposition with thermal oxidation and using atomic layer etching to heal surface damage, the authors significantly enhance the signal-to-noise ratio of the color centers. This progress is vital for integrating high-quality quantum emitters into mature, CMOS-compatible semiconductor platforms.
main_problem: The background photoluminescence (PL) noise caused by conventional CMOS-compatible processing (e.g., plasma etching and PECVD deposition) masks the signal from silicon vacancy (V_Si) color centers in 4H-SiC, degrading their utility for quantum technologies.
main_result: The study demonstrates that using thermally grown oxides with NO annealing and combining RIE with ALE significantly reduces surface noise, achieving a signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of up to 50 for deeper emitters and maintaining high electrical performance in lateral pin-diodes.
method: The researchers compared various surface treatments (PECVD, ALD, thermal oxidation, RIE, ALE, and TMAH etching) using depth-resolved confocal PL spectroscopy, in-line wafer-scale PL mapping, and electrical I-V characterization.
model_or_system: The experimental platform consists of 4H-SiC lateral pin-diodes on both c-plane and a-plane substrates, featuring silicon vacancy (V_Si) color centers generated via electron irradiation.
key_observables: Photoluminescence (PL) intensity, signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), second-order correlation function g(2), blocking voltage, and leakage current.
important_parameters: V_Si emission intensity (~8 kcounts/s), blocking voltage (up to 150 V), leakage current (< 10 pA/µm), and 730 nm excitation wavelength.
main_assumptions: The paraxial approximation used in depth scans may slightly overestimate focal depth due to spherical aberration and high-angle refraction.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows PL noise in standard CMOS processes; Figure 2 uses violin plots to compare various passivation and etching techniques; Figure 3 illustrates PL trends across different processing steps; Figure 4 presents the schematic of pin-diode designs and depth scans; Figure SI 2 compares surface damage between RIE, ALE, and hybrid RIE+ALE processes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of processing-induced noise, details the various surface treatment and fabrication strategies evaluated, presents the comparative results for optical and electrical performance, and concludes with recommendations for optimizing quantum-grade SiC devices.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum information because it provides a practical roadmap for improving the spectral purity and signal strength of solid-state single-photon sources, which is essential for scalable quantum networks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
